annotations.label: Power law

anchor.snippet: a power law is a functional relationship between two quantities

annotations.note: Mathlib has the real power function `Real.rpow` underlying `y = a x^k` but no named definition of a power-law relationship.

annotations.label: Square area as power law

anchor.snippet: the area of a square has a power law relationship with the length of its side

annotations.note: No formalization of the area-of-a-square example as a power-law instance was found.

annotations.label: Scale invariance of power laws

anchor.snippet: scaling the argument

annotations.note: `Real.mul_rpow` gives `(c*x)^k = c^k * x^k`, the algebraic identity underlying scale invariance, but the statement about power laws as the unique scale-invariant relations is not formalized.

annotations.label: Mean and variance existence

anchor.snippet: has a well-defined mean over

annotations.note: No lemma about existence/divergence of mean and variance of power-law distributions was found.

annotations.label: Median of a power law

anchor.snippet: The median does exist

annotations.note: No formula for the median of a power-law distribution was found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Universality class

anchor.snippet: this sharing of dynamics is referred to as universality

annotations.note: Statistical-physics notion of universality classes is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Power law with deviation term

anchor.snippet: an approximation to a power-law

annotations.note: The approximate form `y = a x^k (1 + ε(x))` is not defined as a Mathlib concept.

annotations.label: Truncated power-function distribution

anchor.snippet: a distribution that is a truncated power function is possible

annotations.note: Truncated power-function distributions are not formalized.

annotations.label: Broken power law

anchor.snippet: A broken power law is a piecewise function

annotations.note: Piecewise broken power-law definitions are not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Smoothly broken power law

anchor.snippet: smoothly spliced together to construct a smoothly broken power law

annotations.note: Smoothly-broken power laws are not formalized.

annotations.label: Power law with exponential cutoff

anchor.section: Power law with exponential cutoff (truncated power law)
anchor.snippet: A power law with an exponential cutoff is simply a power law multiplied by an exponential function

annotations.note: The `x^{-α} e^{-βx}` form is not defined as a named Mathlib object.

annotations.label: Curved power law

anchor.snippet: Curved power law

annotations.note: Curved power-law functions are not formalized.

annotations.label: Power-law probability distribution

anchor.snippet: a power-law probability distribution is a distribution whose density function

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes the Pareto pdf and measure, which are the canonical power-law distribution, but not a general `Power-law distribution` type.

annotations.label: Slowly varying function

anchor.snippet: is a slowly varying function

annotations.note: Karamata's notion of a slowly varying function (L(rx)/L(x) → 1) does not appear to be formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Pareto distribution form

anchor.snippet: the power law has the form of the Pareto distribution

annotations.note: `paretoPDFReal`/`paretoMeasure` directly formalize the Pareto density `r t^r x^{-(r+1)}`.

annotations.label: Divergence of moments

anchor.snippet: its moments are given by

annotations.note: No lemma on divergence of moments of Pareto/power-law distributions was found.

annotations.label: Exponential cutoff distribution

anchor.snippet: A modification, which does not satisfy the general form above, with an exponential cutoff

annotations.note: Power-law-with-exponential-cutoff distributions are not formalized.

annotations.label: Tweedie variance-to-mean power law

anchor.snippet: The Tweedie distributions are a family of statistical models

annotations.note: Tweedie distributions are not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Pareto Q–Q plot

anchor.snippet: Pareto Q–Q plots compare the quantiles of the log-transformed data

annotations.note: Q–Q plot diagnostics are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Mean residual life plot

anchor.snippet: the mean residual life plot consists of first log-transforming the data

annotations.note: Mean residual life plots are not formalized.

annotations.label: Log-log plot of power law

anchor.snippet: Taking the logarithm of a power law of the form

annotations.note: `Real.log_rpow` gives `log(x^y) = y * log x`, the identity behind the linear log-log plot, but the plotting interpretation is not formalized.

annotations.label: Survival function representation

anchor.snippet: the survival function

annotations.note: Mathlib has the general cumulative distribution function `ProbabilityTheory.cdf`, from which the survival function `1 − F` can be expressed, but there is no named survival/complementary-CDF decl.

annotations.label: Bundle plot

anchor.snippet: This methodology consists of plotting a bundle for the log-transformed sample

annotations.note: Bundle-plot diagnostics are not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Maximum likelihood estimator (continuous)

anchor.snippet: we fit a power-law distribution of the form

annotations.note: No MLE formula for the continuous power-law exponent was found.

annotations.label: Hill estimator equivalence

anchor.snippet: This estimator is equivalent to the popular

annotations.note: The Hill estimator from extreme value theory is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Maximum likelihood estimator (discrete)

anchor.snippet: the maximum likelihood exponent is the solution to the transcendental equation

annotations.note: No MLE for the discrete power-law/zeta-distribution exponent was found.

annotations.label: Kolmogorov–Smirnov estimation

anchor.snippet: uses the minimization of the Kolmogorov–Smirnov statistic

annotations.note: The Kolmogorov–Smirnov statistic and estimation procedure are not formalized in Mathlib.
